# Silicon photonics for high-speed communications and photonic signal processing

[*npj Nanophotonics*](/npjnanophoton)
**volume 1**, Article number: 27 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

15k Accesses

15 Citations

6 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Leveraging on the mature processing infrastructure of silicon microelectronics, silicon photonic integrated circuits may be readily scaled to large volume production for low-cost high-volume applications such as optical transceivers for data centers. Driven by the rapid growth of generative artificial intelligence and the resultant rapid increase in data traffic in data centers, new integrated optical transceivers will be needed to support multichannel high-capacity communications beyond 1.6Tb/s. In this paper, we review some of the recent advances in high performance optical waveguide grating couplers (WGC) as a key enabling technology for future high capacity communications. We describe the novel use of shifted-polysilicon overlay gratings on top of the silicon grating that enabled foundry manufactured chips to have fiber-chip coupling losses of under 1 dB. The use of mirror symmetry and resonant cavity enhancement in the design of gratings can increase the 1-dB optical bandwidths of grating couplers to over 100 nm. Multimode waveguide grating couplers (MWGC) may be designed for the selective launch of different modes channels in multimode fibers for mode-division-multiplexing (MDM) communications. The use of different modes or polarizations in optical fibers for high capacity communications requires the unscrambling of data lanes which are mixed together during the optical fiber transmission. We describe how silicon photonic circuits can be used to perform unitary matrix operations and unscramble the different data lanes in multichannel optical communication systems. We also describe recent advances on high-speed silicon modulators for enabling data rates of individual data lanes in an integrated optical transceiver beyond 300 Gb/s.

## Introduction

With more than six decades of intensive research and development within the microelectronics industry, one of the most important advantages of silicon is the manufacturing infrastructure and materials processing knowledge that now enables silicon microelectronics to scale to the extremely high-yields needed in the large-volume manufacture of over 60 billion transistors per chip, all functioning flawlessly, in the mass production of microprocessors. Although a cursory glance at the optical properties of silicon, such as its indirect bandgap (inefficient light emission), centrosymmetric crystal structure (zero second-order nonlinearity for use in electro-optic (EO) modulators) shows silicon to be less suitable for discrete photonic devices than other materials, such as III–V semiconductors for lasers, or lithium niobate for optical modulators, the scalable manufacturability of silicon photonics (SiPh), enable many photonic devices to be integrated together with high yield in a photonic circuit, has propelled SiPh to become one of the mainstream technologies for photonic integration. SiPh can integrate the many different functional elements for multichannel optical transceivers and for advanced photonic signal processing applications. In this review, we focus on some of the recent advances in the technology for multichannel communications including advanced WGC for the interface of SiPh chips with optical fibers, and discuss how they can enable the efficient launch of different modes and polarization in optical fiber for high-capacity multi-lane optical communications. We also describe the SiPh based optical signal processors which can be used to unscramble the mixing of data lanes in optical fiber transmission. By implementing coherent photonic circuits which precisely control the phase of the optical signal in the circuit, we can perform matrix operations in the optical domain, and provide a low latency and power efficient alternative to the digital signal processors that have conventionally been used in high capacity optical communication systems. We also review some of the most recent advances in high-speed optical modulators in the SiPh platform. Because of space constraints, we have not included a discussion of the important advances in the heterogeneous integration of lasers with SiPh, which is discussed in other review articles[1](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR1 "Xiang, C. et al. High-performance silicon photonics using heterogeneous integration. IEEE J. Sel. Top. Quantum Electron. 28, 1–15 (2022)."). Also, SiPh-based metasurfaces have been previously investigated[2](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR2 "Arbabi, A., Horie, Y., Bagheri, M. & Faraon, A. Dielectric metasurfaces for complete control of phase and polarization with subwavelength spatial resolution and high transmission. Nat. Nanotechnol. 10, 937–943 (2015)."),[3](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR3 "Wang, S., Wen, S., Deng, Z.-L., Li, X. & Yang, Y. Metasurface-based solid poincare sphere polarizer. Phys. Rev. Lett. 130, 123801 (2023).").

## Advanced waveguide grating couplers

WGCs are fundamental functional elements, widely used device to bridge the fiber-to-chip interface. Compared with edge couplers, GCs have the advantages of arbitrary positioning on the chip surface, wafer-scale testing, relaxed fabrication and alignment tolerances, and simpler fabrication without the need for deep facet etch or facet polishing. Also, using the compact focusing design, the design layout of GCs can form two dimensional array, not easily possible with edge couplers, for interface with multicore fibers. GCs can offer sub-decibel coupling losses[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022)."),[5](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR5 "Ding, Y., Peucheret, C., Ou, H. & Yvind, K. Fully etched apodized grating coupler on the SOI platform with -0.58 dB coupling efficiency. Opt. Lett. 39, 5348–5350 (2014)."), broad operational bandwidth[6](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR6 "Zhou, X. T. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated broadband waveguide grating couplers With 1 dB bandwidth over 100 nm. IEEE Photonics J 16, 1–6 (2024)."), dual polarization coupling[7](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR7 "Zhou, X., Hu, G., Qin, Y. & Tsang, H. K. Polarization-independent waveguide grating coupler using an optimized polysilicon overlay. Opt. Lett. 47, 5825–5828 (2022)."),[8](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR8 "Zhou, X., Tsang, H. K., High performance dual polarization and dual wavelength band waveguide grating coupler. In Proc. Asia Communications and Photonics Conference (ACP), 1384–1386 (2022)."), wavelength demultiplexing[9](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR9 "Roelkens, G., Van Thourhout, D. & Baets, R. Silicon-on-insulator ultra-compact duplexer based on a diffractive grating structure. Opt. Express 15, 10091–10096 (2007)."),[10](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR10 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Combined polysilicon and silicon gratings for dual-wavelength-band waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 48, 279–282 (2023)."), perfectly vertical coupling[11](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR11 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Optimized shift-pattern overlay for high coupling efficiency waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 47, 3968–3971 (2022).") for multicore fiber and multimode[12](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR12 "Zhou, X. T. & Tsang, H. K. High efficiency multimode waveguide grating coupler for few-mode fibers. IEEE Photonics J 14, 1–5 (2022).") coupling for space division multiplexing. In the following section we review some of the recent advances in the technology of GCs.

### High coupling efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler

To realize high coupling efficiency (CE) of WGC, both the directionality and mode matching of the WGC should be improved. Early approaches to realize high CE grating coupler include using the apodization structure[13](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR13 "Chen, X., Li, C., Fung, C. K. Y., Lo, S. M. G. & Tsang, H. K. Apodized waveguide grating couplers for efficient coupling to optical fibers. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 22, 1156–1158 (2010)."), polysilicon overlay structure[14](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR14 "Roelkens, G., Van Thourhout, D. & Baets, R. High efficiency silicon-on-Insulator grating coupler based on a poly-silicon overlay. Opt. Express 14, 11622–11630 (2006)."),[15](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR15 "Vermeulen, D. et al. High-efficiency fiber-to-chip grating couplers realized using an advanced CMOS-compatible silicon-on-insulator platform. Opt. Express 18, 18278–18283 (2010)."), subwavelength structure with metal reflector structure[16](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR16 "Mangal, N., Missinne, J., Van Steenberge, G., Van Campenhout, J. & Snyder, B. Performance evaluation of backside emitting O-Band grating couplers for 100-μm-thick silicon photonics interposers. IEEE Photonics J 11, 1–11 (2019)."), grating with bottom Bragg reflector[17](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR17 "Taillaert, D. et al. Grating couplers for coupling between optical fibers and nanophotonic waveguides. Jpn. Appl. Phys. Part 1-Regul. Pap. Brief Commun. Rev. Pap. 45, 6071–6077 (2006)."), using thick waveguide layer[18](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR18 "Marchetti, R. et al. High-efficiency grating-couplers: demonstration of a new design strategy. Sci. Rep. 7, 16670 (2017)."), dual-level grating structure[19](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR19 "Sacher, W. D. et al. Wide bandwidth and high coupling efficiency Si3N4-on-SOI dual-level grating coupler. Opt. Express 22, 10938–10947 (2014)."), interleaved trenches structure[20](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR20 "Alonso-Ramos, C. et al. Fiber-chip grating coupler based on interleaved trenches with directionality exceeding 95. Opt. Lett. 39, 5351–5354 (2014).") to realize high CE grating coupler.

However, most of these early approaches require a tiny feature size as small as 40 nm, possible with electron beam lithography[5](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR5 "Ding, Y., Peucheret, C., Ou, H. & Yvind, K. Fully etched apodized grating coupler on the SOI platform with -0.58 dB coupling efficiency. Opt. Lett. 39, 5348–5350 (2014)."),[21](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR21 "Zaoui, W. S. et al. Bridging the gap between optical fibers and silicon photonic integrated circuits. Opt. Express 22, 1277–1286 (2014)."),[22](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR22 "Hoppe, N. et al. Ultra-efficient silicon-on-insulator grating couplers with backside metal mirrors. IEEE J. Sel. Top. Quantum Electron. 26, 1–6 (2020).") but below the limitations of the 193 nm deep ultraviolet (DUV) photolithography typically used in silicon photonic foundries.

We recently proposed a new conceptual approach for the design of a high CE grating coupler with a large minimum feature size (MFS) above 180 nm which is compatible with the design rules of many commercial foundries. Unlike the previous theoretical proposal of a dual-layer grating with top and bottom etching of the silicon waveguide layer[23](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR23 "Dai, M. et al. Highly efficient and perfectly vertical chip-to-fiber dual-layer grating coupler. Opt. Express 23, 1691–1698 (2015)."), our proposed approach can be implemented by using a polysilicon layer deposited on the silicon grating.

The conceptual operation of the high CE grating structure is shown in Fig. [1](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig1). The optimized pattern and shifted positioning of the polysilicon overlay layer above the lower silicon grating structure makes it possible to engineer constructive interference for the upward diffracted light and destructive interference for the downward diffracted light, thus improving the directionality. A shift in position between the two layers also produces four subwavelength structures that enable a gradual change in the effective index, which can effectively provide a blazing effect of grating, and thus realize a high CE for both off-vertical angled and perfectly vertical grating couplers, as shown in Fig. [1c](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig1). The two layers combine to form subwavelength structures which enables the precise design of the grating strength of each period to further improve the mode matching and hence the overall CE.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44310-024-00024-7/MediaObjects/44310_2024_24_Fig1_HTML.png)

**a** cross-section view of the grating coupler[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022)."). **b**, **c** Four types of subwavelength structures in the vertical direction and their effective index[11](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR11 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Optimized shift-pattern overlay for high coupling efficiency waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 47, 3968–3971 (2022).") (**a**) Reproduced with permission[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") Copyright 2022, IEEE. **b**, **c** Reproduced with permission[11](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR11 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Optimized shift-pattern overlay for high coupling efficiency waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 47, 3968–3971 (2022).") Copyright 2022, Optica publishing group.

In the design, the MFS is 180 nm, both the shift, period and duty cycle of each period in each layer are independently optimized using genetic numerical optimization algorithm[24](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR24 "Zhou, X., Tsang, H. K., Multimode waveguide grating couplers for mode division multiplexing in multi-mode fibers. In Proc. Asia Communications and Photonics Conference (ACP), 1–3 (IEEE, 2021).") to improve the CE. To reduce the simulation time, 2D FDTD simulation is used for the design optimization and 3D FDTD simulation is used to confirm the final simulation result.

To attain sub-decibel CE in the silicon WGC, we introduced a shift *ds* in the starting position of the polysilicon grating layer relative to the silicon grating layer in the optimization, see Fig. [1a](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig1). The initial shift is needed to provide constructive interference upwards and destructive interference downward between the upper polysilicon and the lower silicon layer, thus improving the directionality of the grating, and also helps reduce the grating strength in the first few periods of the grating to improve mode-matching with the fiber mode.

The simulation of the optimized grating coupler shows −0.68 dB CE at 1550.5 nm and 1 dB bandwidth of 33 nm. The design was fabricated, and the peak CE was measured as −0.89 dB at 1553.4 nm with 1 dB optical bandwidth of 34.8 nm (1537.6 nm to 1572.4 nm), as shown in Fig. [2](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig2).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44310-024-00024-7/MediaObjects/44310_2024_24_Fig2_HTML.png)

Simulation and experiment result of the sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide GC[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") Reproduced with permission[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") Copyright 2022, IEEE.

Table [1](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Tab1) compares these recent results with previously reported high-efficiency grating coupler designs. The main advance is that the sub-decibel efficiency to standard single mode fiber (SSMF) was achieved using a more robust design with a MFS above 170 nm, that was fabricated by 193 nm DUV photolithography.

Instead of the high CE, fabrication tolerance is also important in manufacturability. Figure [3](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig3) shows the fabrication tolerance of the shifted polysilicon overlay GC under different fabrication variations.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44310-024-00024-7/MediaObjects/44310_2024_24_Fig3_HTML.png)

**a** Thickness tolerance. **b** Shift tolerance. **c** Etch depth tolerance. **d** Etch width tolerance[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") Reproduced with permission[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") Copyright 2022, IEEE.

It can be seen from Fig. [3](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig3) that the CE of the GC remains above 80% (−0.969 dB) CE for thickness error variations of −60 nm to +43 nm, shift error variations of ±40 nm, etch depth variation of −30 nm to +18 nm and etch width variations of ±60 nm. The numerical analysis in Fig. [3](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig3) shows that the high efficiency GC has excellent fabrication tolerance, and the design is suitable for large volume production. The simulated tolerance of the performance of the proposed GCs (Fig. [3](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig3)) is consistent with the measured performance among different chips[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022)."),[6](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR6 "Zhou, X. T. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated broadband waveguide grating couplers With 1 dB bandwidth over 100 nm. IEEE Photonics J 16, 1–6 (2024)."),[11](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR11 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Optimized shift-pattern overlay for high coupling efficiency waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 47, 3968–3971 (2022).") fabricated by the foundry.

### Dual polarization waveguide grating coupler

The TE and TM mode in the silicon waveguide have a significant difference in effective refractive index, causing different center wavelengths for a given grating period for the TE and TM modes. Most GCs are designed to operate with only one polarization, either TE mode or TM mode. Polarization-independent GCs that can couple a randomly polarized input in the optical fiber with the PIC are needed for optical receivers and they can also enable the use of polarization division multiplexing (PDM). Typical methods to realize polarization-independent GCs include 1D GC with single port[25](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR25 "Song, J. H. et al. Polarization-independent nonuniform grating couplers on silicon-on-insulator. Opt. Lett. 40, 3941–3944 (2015)."),[26](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR26 "Chen, X. & Tsang, H. K. Polarization-independent grating couplers for silicon-on-insulator nanophotonic waveguides. Opt. Lett. 36, 796–798 (2011)."), 1D GC with dual port[27](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR27 "Tang, Y. B., Dai, D. X. & He, S. L. Proposal for a grating waveguide serving as both a polarization splitter and an efficient coupler for silicon-on-insulator nanophotonic circuits. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 21, 242–244 (2009)."), and 2D GC[28](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR28 "Bogaerts, W. et al. A polarization-diversity wavelength duplexer circuit in silicon-on-insulator photonic wires. Opt. Express 15, 1567–1578 (2007)."),[29](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR29 "Watanabe, T., Fedoryshyn, Y. & Leuthold, J. 2-D grating couplers for vertical fiber coupling in two polarizations. IEEE Photonics J 11, 1–9 (2019).").

Recently, we demonstrated a high-performance 1D GC that can couple both TE and TM modes into a single waveguide. The conceptual operation of the polarization independent GC is shown in Fig. [4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig4): The structure here can be regarded as a composite grating with two different gratings.

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44310-024-00024-7/MediaObjects/44310_2024_24_Fig4_HTML.png)

**a** 3D view of the perfectly vertical coupling polarization independent GC. **b** 2D cross-section view of the proposed GC. **c**, **d** Experiment results for the proposed polarization independent GC[7](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR7 "Zhou, X., Hu, G., Qin, Y. & Tsang, H. K. Polarization-independent waveguide grating coupler using an optimized polysilicon overlay. Opt. Lett. 47, 5825–5828 (2022).") Reproduced with permission[7](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR7 "Zhou, X., Hu, G., Qin, Y. & Tsang, H. K. Polarization-independent waveguide grating coupler using an optimized polysilicon overlay. Opt. Lett. 47, 5825–5828 (2022).") Copyright 2022, Optica publishing group.

Polarization independence was achieved by initially setting the periods of GC1 and GC2 for operation with TM (GC1) and TE (GC2), respectively. Then combining the two designs together, the upper polysilicon layer has the same period as GC1 for TM polarization, and the lower silicon grating layer has the same period as GC2 for TE polarization. This composite grating, GC3, can work for both TE and TM polarizations but has a relatively low CE. Using GC3 as an optimization start point, further optimization of each period/duty cycle to improve both the CE and polarization independence and get the final optimized composite grating GC4 suitable for dual polarizations (DP). The simulations predicted −3.6 dB and −3.5 dB coupling losses for the TE and TM polarization, while the perfectly vertical GC measurements had CE of −3.96 dB for TE polarization and −3.93 dB for TM polarization.

Instead of the single port 1D GC for dual polarization, dual port GCs can also be used to realize dual polarization coupling. One port is for TE polarization, and another port is for TM polarization as has been proposed[27](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR27 "Tang, Y. B., Dai, D. X. & He, S. L. Proposal for a grating waveguide serving as both a polarization splitter and an efficient coupler for silicon-on-insulator nanophotonic circuits. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 21, 242–244 (2009).") and experimentally demonstrated[30](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR30 "Zaoui, W. S., Kunze, A., Vogel, W. & Berroth, M. CMOS-compatible polarization splitting grating couplers with a backside metal mirror. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 25, 1395–1397 (2013)."). Early designs require electron-beam lithography (EBL) fabrication and a bottom metal reflector to improve the CE to −3.8 dB. However, our recent innovation using the polysilicon overlay builds upon the previous theory[27](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR27 "Tang, Y. B., Dai, D. X. & He, S. L. Proposal for a grating waveguide serving as both a polarization splitter and an efficient coupler for silicon-on-insulator nanophotonic circuits. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 21, 242–244 (2009).") to realize the dual port coupling of two different polarizations into two opposite directions (see Fig. [5](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig5)), and the engineering of optimized shift-pattern polysilicon overlay yielded much higher CEs[11](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR11 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Optimized shift-pattern overlay for high coupling efficiency waveguide grating couplers. Opt. Lett. 47, 3968–3971 (2022)."),[12](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR12 "Zhou, X. T. & Tsang, H. K. High efficiency multimode waveguide grating coupler for few-mode fibers. IEEE Photonics J 14, 1–5 (2022)."). The schematic of the dual polarization GC is shown in Fig. [5a](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig5). The device can couple the orthogonal polarizations into two opposite directions with predicted coupling efficiencies of −1.46 dB and −1.88 dB for the TE and TM polarization and MFS of 181 nm.

![figure 5](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44310-024-00024-7/MediaObjects/44310_2024_24_Fig5_HTML.png)

**a** cross-section view of the dual polarization GC. **b**, **c** Simulated CE for the TE and TM mode[8](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR8 "Zhou, X., Tsang, H. K., High performance dual polarization and dual wavelength band waveguide grating coupler. In Proc. Asia Communications and Photonics Conference (ACP), 1384–1386 (2022).") Reproduced with permission[8](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR8 "Zhou, X., Tsang, H. K., High performance dual polarization and dual wavelength band waveguide grating coupler. In Proc. Asia Communications and Photonics Conference (ACP), 1384–1386 (2022).") Copyright 2022, IEEE.

Compared with the 1D single port and dual port dual polarization GC, two-dimensional GC can couple the orthogonal modes in the fiber to the same single polarization in two silicon waveguides and avoid the use of polarization rotation devices. 2D polarization-diversity GCs have been proposed and demonstrated by many groups[28](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR28 "Bogaerts, W. et al. A polarization-diversity wavelength duplexer circuit in silicon-on-insulator photonic wires. Opt. Express 15, 1567–1578 (2007)."),[29](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR29 "Watanabe, T., Fedoryshyn, Y. & Leuthold, J. 2-D grating couplers for vertical fiber coupling in two polarizations. IEEE Photonics J 11, 1–9 (2019)."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Carroll, L., Gerace, D., Cristiani, I. & Andreani, L. C. Optimizing polarization-diversity couplers for Si-photonics: reaching the -1dB coupling efficiency threshold. Opt. Express 22, 14769–14781 (2014)."),[32](#ref-CR32 "Ruiz, J. L. P. et al. Compact dual-polarization silicon integrated couplers for multicore fibers. Opt. Lett. 46, 3649–3652 (2021)."),[33](#ref-CR33 "Zou, J., Yu, Y. & Zhang, X. Single step etched two dimensional grating coupler based on the SOI platform. Opt. Express 23, 32490–32495 (2015)."),[34](#ref-CR34 "Zhang, Z. Y. et al. Two-dimensional apodized grating coupler for polarization-independent and surface-normal optical coupling. J. Lightw. Technol. 38, 4037–4044 (2020)."),[35](#ref-CR35 "Taillaert, D. et al. A compact two-dimensional grating coupler used as a polarization splitter. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 15, 1249–1251 (2003)."),[36](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR36 "Luo, Y. et al. Low-loss two-dimensional silicon photonic grating coupler with a backside metal mirror. Opt. Lett. 43, 474–477 (2018)."). However, the devices fabricated by DUV photolithography in commercial foundry typically had relatively high coupling loss of 6 dB when manufactured with MFS above 180 nm[37](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR37 "Yi, D., Wu, X. R. & Tsang, H. K. Ultra-compact polarization analyzer based on micro-ring resonators. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 33, 1371–1374 (2021)."). We recently introduced a novel design for a perfectly-vertical polarization diversity two-dimensional WGC with high efficiency and low polarization-dependent loss (PDL). The basic approach used for dual polarization coupling (see Fig. [6](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#Fig6)) is based on combining the polarization diversity two-dimensional WGC[28](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR28 "Bogaerts, W. et al. A polarization-diversity wavelength duplexer circuit in silicon-on-insulator photonic wires. Opt. Express 15, 1567–1578 (2007).") with the optimized shift pattern polysilicon overlay method to realize high CE[4](/articles/s44310-024-00024-7#ref-CR4 "Zhou, X. & Tsang, H. K. Photolithography fabricated sub-decibel high-efficiency silicon waveguide grating coupler. IEEE Photonics Technol. Lett. 35, 43–46 (2022).") and implement

![Team achieves world’s fastest data transmission rate using photonics](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.interestingengineering.com%2F2024%2F02%2F01%2Fimage%2Fjpeg%2Fibw6pFFpZ89QnsLNCEWxiDFqHR9HOLNvxG2Ws9xP.jpg&w=1200&q=75)

A conceptual image of data transmission

[nadla/iStock](https://www.istockphoto.com/photo/binary-code-gm1175137428-327110358)

[nadla/iStock](https://www.istockphoto.com/photo/binary-code-gm1175137428-327110358)

From the cozy corners of neighborhood libraries where students connect their laptops to the Wi-Fi network to the remote outposts in the Antarctic where scientists monitor weather, [wireless communication](https://interestingengineering.com/innovation/high-power-laser-powers-nanosatellite "wireless communication") is the hero of our connected world.

While we marvel at the speed of our current networks, researchers worldwide are already looking beyond 5G to shape the future of communication in 6G networks. You can imagine a world where instant communication powers virtual reality experiences or enables surgeons to control robots from miles away.

To turn these dreams into reality, researchers need wireless channels with ultra-high data speeds, and a recent study published in IEICE Electronics Express unveils a promising breakthrough using lasers to achieve just that.

A team of researchers from Osaka University and IMRA AMERICA has shattered records by harnessing the power of lasers to enhance data transmission speeds. The trick lies in reducing system noise, a feat achieved by utilizing photonics in a sub-terahertz band ranging from 100 GHz to 300 GHz.

![](https://images.interestingengineering.com/2024/02/01/image/png/sUB35qCOPbLN2AQFrIeZbMIV8bUeHYTVMX4JHSFc.png)

[Osaka University](https://resou.osaka-u.ac.jp/en/research/2024/20240131_1)

For 6G networks to deliver near-instantaneous communication, researchers rely on a sophisticated technique known as “multi-level signal modulation.” This method allows for cramming large volumes of data into the sub-terahertz band while maintaining swift response times.

However, operating at the upper echelons of these high frequencies poses a challenge. Multi-level signal modulation is highly sensitive to noise, especially when dealing with precise reference signals. The team led by Keisuke Maekawa found a solution to this bottleneck by leveraging a photonic device-based signal generator.

Keisuke Maekawa, lead author of the study, explains, “This problem has limited 300-GHz communications so far. However, we found that at high frequencies, a signal generator based on a photonic device had much less phase noise than a conventional electrical signal generator.”

The researchers utilized a stimulated Brillouin scattering laser, which exploits interactions between sound and light waves, to generate an incredibly precise signal. Implementing this laser-based signal generator in both the transmitter and receiver of a 300 GHz-band wireless communication system, they achieved a single-channel transmission rate of a staggering 240 gigabits per second.

Tadao Nagatsuma, the project’s Principal Investigator, notes that this achievement represents the highest transmission rate ever recorded globally using online digital signal processing (DSP).

#### RECOMMENDED ARTICLES

As the world continues to embrace 5G technology, researchers are diligently laying the groundwork for 6G. The results of this study mark a significant leap toward realizing 300GHz-band wireless communication.

The researchers envision that, with the application of multiplexing techniques and the development of more sensitive receivers, the data rate could skyrocket to 1 terabit per second. This achievement would usher in a new era of almost instantaneous global communication, transforming how we connect and interact globally.

The [study](https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/elex/advpub/0/advpub_20.20230584/_article "study") was published in the *IEICE Electronics Express* journal.

#### ABOUT THE AUTHOR

[**Abdul-Rahman Oladimeji Bello**](https://interestingengineering.com/author/abdul-rahman-oladimeji-bello) Abdul-Rahman is a seasoned SEO writer and journalist with over seven years of experience spanning different writing spheres. His proficiency reaches beyond words as he holds a Banking & Finance degree, Graduate Certificates in International Business Management and Supply Chain Management, and a Diploma in Accounting and Business. Yet, he understands that science and engineering are essential to keep the wheel of innovation running. His vast knowledge encompasses tech, finance, environmental issues, science, engineering, and politics. An enthusiastic coffee lover, he relishes the bold taste of a quality brew every morning, starting his day on a vibrant note.

# How Photonics is Revolutionizing Data Transfer

## The increasing demand for accelerated data processing and transmission pushes conventional electronics to their limits.

![How Photonics is Revolutionizing Data Transfer ](/newstransfer/upload/450x308_EiIe.jpg)

**By**

Semiconductor Review | Wednesday, June 26, 2024

Stay ahead of the industry with exclusive feature stories on the top companies, expert insights and the latest news delivered straight to your inbox. [Subscribe today.](/subscribe/)

**Photonics enhances speed, efficiency, and size in sectors like telecommunications, data centers, biophotonics, quantum computing, silicon photonics, and metamaterials, reducing power consumption and electromagnetic interference.**

**FREMONT, CA:** The increasing demand for accelerated data processing and transmission pushes conventional electronics to their limits. Photonics emerges as a transformative technology capable of revolutionizing information transfer. By leveraging light (photons) rather than electrons, photonics offers substantial advantages over traditional electronics, particularly in speed, efficiency, and size.

**Unveiling the Speed of Light: Advancing Data Transfer**

Photonics excels in transmitting data at unprecedented speeds. Unlike electrons, which encounter resistance in wires, photons move through optical fibers almost at the speed of light. This capability translates into tangible benefits for data centers, telecommunication networks, and high-performance computing. For example, photonic interconnects efficiently manage vast data volumes generated by artificial intelligence and cloud computing, facilitating faster processing and real-time analysis.

**Energy Efficiency: A Sustainable Advantage**

In contrast to the energy losses inherent in traditional electronics, photonic systems are inherently more energy-efficient. Light travels through optical fibers with minimal energy dissipation, significantly reducing power consumption. This characteristic is particularly critical for data centers, a sector with increasing global energy demands. As data requirements escalate, the energy-saving potential of photonics becomes increasingly pivotal.

**Miniaturization: Redefining Device Design**

Photonics offers compelling miniaturization prospects for next-generation electronic devices. Photonic integrated circuits (PICs) integrate diverse optical components such as lasers, waveguides, and modulators onto a single chip. This level of miniaturization surpasses what traditional electronics can achieve, enabling the creation of smaller, denser, and more powerful devices. Photonics' miniaturization capabilities are especially relevant for applications in wearable electronics and internet-of-things (IoT) devices, where size and power efficiency are paramount.

Photonics is making significant strides across various sectors, demonstrating its transformative impact through real-world applications and cutting-edge research. Photonics underpins modern fiber optic networks in telecommunications, facilitating high-speed internet and efficient data transmission over long distances. Within data centers, photonics-based enhance server connectivity, enabling rapid data exchange and real-time analytics. Biophotonics leverages light for medical imaging and diagnostics, exemplified by technologies like optical coherence tomography for non-invasive eye imaging and biosensors for disease detection. Photonics also plays a pivotal role in quantum computing by encoding quantum information with photons, promising scientific computation and materials science breakthroughs. In research, ongoing advancements in silicon photonics promise to revolutionize electronic circuit design by integrating photonic components onto silicon chips, while non-linear photonics explores new light-matter interactions for enhanced signal processing capabilities. Furthermore, metamaterials are opening avenues for novel photonic devices, such as cloaking and ultra-compact optical circuits, showcasing photonics' potential for groundbreaking innovation.

Photonics presents advantages beyond speed, efficiency, and miniaturization. Photonic systems are less vulnerable to electromagnetic interference (EMI), which can disrupt electronic signals. Moreover, photons enable parallel data transmission, allowing multiple data streams to traverse a single channel simultaneously, enhancing overall data throughput.

Photonics stands poised to transform information transmission and processing. With its superior speed, efficiency, and miniaturization capabilities, photonics is a cornerstone in advancing next-generation technologies. As research and development endeavors continue, photonics is expected to play an increasingly pivotal role in shaping the future landscape of electronics, from data centers to everyday devices.

--- SOURCE 5: Photonics Speeds Up Data Center AI ---
URL: https://semiengineering.com/photonics-speeds-up-data-center-ai/

SUMMARY:
Today, pluggable optical transceivers enable data transmission between NICs and switches across tens of meters. As data rates escalate, these

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/semi_logo.webp)

# Photonics Speeds Up Data Center AI

Highlights from the Optical Fiber Communications Conference 2025.

![popularity](https://semiengineering.com/wp-content/themes/se_current/images/pop_ratings/pop_rating_lev4.png "popularity")

Photonics is playing an increasingly vital role in the acceleration of AI within data centers.

The global market for optical components is already substantial, accounting for $17 billion in revenue last year. Historically, telecommunications — such as undersea cables and fiber-to-the-home — dominated demand. However, the datacom sector, especially AI-driven data centers, now accounts for more than 60% of the market. This shift is accelerating the growth of optical technologies.

To keep up with the increasing performance of AI compute clusters (XPUs, including GPUs and custom accelerators), optical transmission rates are rising rapidly.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/optical1a.png?resize=1339%2C590&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 1: Optical components market history and forecast. Source: OMDIA/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/optical1a.png?resize=1339%2C590&ssl=1)

According to J.P. Morgan, the largest optical component suppliers are Coherent and Innolight (each with 20% market share), followed by Broadcom at 10%. Numerous smaller suppliers also are contributing to the expanding ecosystem.

### AI data center growth driven by LLMs

Large language models (LLMs) are driving exponential growth in AI workloads. As AI capabilities advance and costs decrease, demand is surging. The increasing size of LLMs necessitates massive clusters of XPUs. Interconnect requirements grow faster than the number of XPUs themselves, creating urgent demand for high-bandwidth, low-latency networking solutions.

Broadcom CEO Hock Tan noted that networking costs in data centers are climbing, from 5% to 10% of capital expenditures today to an expected 15% to 20% by 2030.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-8.39.51%E2%80%AFAM.png?resize=2400%2C1124&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 2: AI clusters exploding in size. Source: Dell’Oro Group/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-8.39.51%E2%80%AFAM.png?resize=2400%2C1124&ssl=1)

Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), for example, is deploying clusters with 131,000 Nvidia Blackwell GPUs interconnected via NVLink72.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/optics1.png?resize=919%2C485&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 3: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure supercluster offerings for generative AI. Source: Oracle/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/optics1.png?resize=919%2C485&ssl=1)

### **Scale-Out vs. Scale-Up Networking**

In AI data centers, there are two main types of interconnects:

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.32.02%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2240%2C1034&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 4: Optics in the data center. Source: Coherent/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.32.02%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2240%2C1034&ssl=1)

While scale-out networking is already optical, the transition to photonics for scale-up networking is underway but not yet complete.

# 

### Optical advancements in scale-out networks

Photonics are central to scale-out architectures. Today, pluggable optical transceivers enable data transmission between NICs and switches across tens of meters. As data rates escalate, these solutions face increasing power and performance limitations.

Oracle’s 131K-GPU fabric uses optical links at all three levels of its scale-out network. However, traditional pluggable optics consume significant power.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.34.41%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2290%2C1168&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 5: Oracle optical cluster network fabric. Source: Oracle/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.34.41%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2290%2C1168&ssl=1)

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.38.22%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2168%2C1016&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 6: Power and TCO remain primary concerns. Source: Meta/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.38.22%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2168%2C1016&ssl=1)

As data rates in the scale-out network increase to keep up with LLM growth and throughput needs, the network power is exceeding the accelerator rack power. Shifting from pluggable optics to CPO (co-packaged optics) can greatly cut the power of the optics from 30W to 9W for a 1.6Tbps link, according to Nvidia.

At GTC25, Nvidia introduced its first scale-out switch with CPO. The power savings enable higher GPU density — up to 3X more GPUs within the same data center power envelope.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.40.27%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2220%2C1022&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 7: 3.5X power saving with Spectrum-X photonics. Source: Nvidia/GTC25**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/Screenshot-2025-04-30-at-1.40.27%E2%80%AFPM.png?resize=2220%2C1022&ssl=1)

Reliability is a key consideration in moving from copper to optics to CPO. Volumes in an AI data center are huge and ramp fast, like iPhones. Yields and reliability must be very high from the stat. Google’s director of platform optics said a link failure rate of 0.004% per day sounds pretty good, but for 1M links that’s 40 link failures/day. Optical solutions need to be engineered for very low failure rates, tested at very demanding levels and with very large sample sizes to ensure that production ramps are successful.

### The path toward CPO in scale-up networks

Scale-up interconnects remain copper-based for now. Nvidia’s Blackwell architecture employs NVLink72, an all-copper solution, with extensive cabling visible across boards, switches, and rack backplanes. Signal frequencies are now so high that copper bundles connect directly to GPUs, bypassing traditional PCB traces.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/8a.png?resize=1430%2C806&ssl=1)  
![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/8b.png?resize=1430%2C797&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 8: Nvidia’s roadmap extends to NVLink576, which still uses copper, but escalating data rates and signal integrity issues will ultimately necessitate optical solutions. Source: Nvidia/GTC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/8a.png?resize=1430%2C806&ssl=1)
![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/8b.png?resize=1430%2C797&ssl=1)

However, copper’s limitations are becoming more pronounced. Nvidia’s roadmap extends to NVLink576, which still uses copper, but escalating data rates and signal integrity issues will ultimately necessitate optical solutions.

Microsoft presented their CPO requirements for future AI accelerators. They want a single physical layer with configurable interfaces to replace the existing interfaces.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/9.png?resize=1279%2C522&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 9: New interconnect scenarios call for unified interfaces with tighter latency and reliability requirements. Source: Microsoft/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/9.png?resize=1279%2C522&ssl=1)

The new unified interface needs to have the “best of both worlds” – the combined specification is the better of the traditional interfaces they are replacing. This makes it more challenging for CPO but increases the market.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/10.png?resize=1430%2C800&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 10: A new unified interface needs to be be better than the traditional interfaces it replaces. Source: Microsoft/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/10.png?resize=1430%2C800&ssl=1)

Nvidia also presented its requirements on CPO integration with AI Accelerators:

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/11.png?resize=1430%2C797&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 11: Nvidia’s CPO requirements. Source: Nvidia/OFC**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/11.png?resize=1430%2C797&ssl=1)

These are challenging but doable requirements.  Needham & Company suggests that the initial move to CPO in Scale-Up networks will occur between racks within a single GPU domain, while intra-rack connections remain copper for the time being.

100% of data center AI chips are made by TSMC. They are deeply involved with the technology roadmaps of all of the major AI players: they only develop what their major customers need. TSMC at their annual technology conference in late April showed their roadmap for AI chips includes co-packaged optics: they see it coming and are getting ready.

### Market outlook and industry players

The transition to CPO in Scale-Up networking is expected to begin within the next few years, with widespread replacement of pluggable optics in the 2030s. The CPO market will grow from zero today to $5 billion by 2030. Early entrants such as Broadcom, Marvell, Ayar Labs, Celestial AI, and Lightmatter are poised to benefit, as well as the laser suppliers like Coherent.

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/12.png?resize=1430%2C791&ssl=1)  
**Fig. 12: Optics growing fast with CPO emerging in 2027-2030. Source: LightCounting/Coherent**

![](https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/12.png?resize=1430%2C791&ssl=1)

Photonics is no longer just enabling AI. It is becoming indispensable to its growth at scale. By the mid 2030s, all interconnects will be optical and all will be CPO.

### Geoff Tate

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in photonics enable data transmission rates exceeding 300 Gb/s, with potential breakthroughs reaching 1.6 Tb/s, significantly enhancing communication speed in telecom networks.

Utilizing multi-level signal modulation techniques, photonics facilitates efficient data encoding, allowing for higher bandwidth utilization and improved signal integrity in telecommunications systems.
Photonics technology supports parallel data transmission, increasing overall network bandwidth and enabling the handling of massive data volumes generated by AI and cloud computing demands.
